description: Phrases for hotels, hostels, and accommodation situations

items.english: I would like to book a room.
items.notes: 'Varata' means 'to book/reserve'. 'Huoneen' is the genitive/accusative of 'huone' (room). The conditional 'haluaisin' makes the request polite. Many Finnish hotels can also be booked online.

items.literalTranslation: Is you-on(adessive) free(partitive plural) rooms(partitive plural)?
items.notes: 'Vapaita' is the partitive plural of 'vapaa' (free/available). 'Huoneita' is the partitive plural of 'huone'. The partitive plural is used because we're asking about an unspecified number of rooms.
'Yhden' is the genitive of 'yksi' (one). 'Hengen' is the genitive of 'henki' (person/spirit, here meaning 'person'). This is the standard way to request a single room in Finnish hotels.
'Kahden' is the genitive of 'kaksi' (two). Same structure as single room. You can specify bed type: 'parisängyllä' (with a double bed) or 'erillisillä vuoteilla' (with separate beds).
'Yö' means 'night'. 'Maksaa' means 'to cost'. Finnish hotel prices typically include VAT (ALV). Breakfast may or may not be included, so always ask.

'Sisältyykö' is from 'sisältyä' (to be included), with the question suffix '-kö'. 'Aamiainen' means 'breakfast'. Finnish hotel breakfasts are typically generous buffets with bread, porridge, cold cuts, and coffee.
'Mihin aikaan' means 'at what time'. 'Sisäänkirjautuminen' is a compound word from 'sisään' (in) + 'kirjautuminen' (registration/logging in). Check-in in Finnish hotels is typically at 14:00-16:00.
'Uloskirjautuminen' is a compound word from 'ulos' (out) + 'kirjautuminen' (registration). Check-out in Finnish hotels is typically by 11:00-12:00.
'Hissi' means 'elevator/lift'. Most Finnish hotels have elevators. Ground floor is called 'ensimmäinen kerros' (first floor) or sometimes '0. kerros'.

items.english: There's a problem with my room.
items.notes: 'Huoneessani' is the inessive case of 'huone' with the possessive suffix '-ni' (my). 'Ongelma' means 'problem'. Use this to start a complaint at the front desk ('vastaanotto').
'Ylimääräisen' is the genitive/accusative of 'ylimääräinen' (extra). 'Tyynyn' is the genitive/accusative of 'tyyny' (pillow). Finnish hotels generally provide high-quality bedding.
'Ilmastointi' means 'air conditioning'. 'Ei toimi' means 'doesn't work' (negative of 'toimia'). Note that many older Finnish hotels may not have AC, as summer temperatures are usually mild.

items.english: I'd like a wake-up call at seven.
items.notes: 'Herätys' means 'wake-up (call)'. 'Kello seitsemältä' means 'at seven o'clock' using the ablative case for time. Finnish uses 24-hour clock in formal settings, so 7 AM would be 'kello 7' and 7 PM 'kello 19'.
'Ilmainen' means 'free (of charge)'. Most Finnish hotels offer free wifi. You can also ask 'Mikä on wifi-salasana?' (What is the wifi password?).
'Uima-allas' is a compound word from 'uima' (swimming) + 'allas' (pool/basin). Many Finnish hotels also have a sauna ('sauna'), which is a must-try cultural experience.
'Voisinko' is the conditional of 'voida' with question suffix '-ko', meaning 'could I'. 'Avaimen' is the genitive/accusative of 'avain' (key). Most Finnish hotels now use key cards ('avainkortti').

items.english: I'd like to extend my stay.
items.notes: 'Jatkaa' means 'to continue/extend'. 'Varaustani' is the partitive of 'varaus' (reservation) with possessive suffix '-ni' (my). Subject to availability.
'Pysäköidä' means 'to park'. Hotel parking in Finland may be free or paid. In cities, parking is often in underground garages ('parkkihalli'). Look for the blue 'P' sign.
'Voitteko' is the formal/plural 'can you' from 'voida'. 'Kutsua' means 'to call/summon'. Finnish taxis can also be ordered via apps like Taksi Helsinki or Uber (available in larger cities).
'Majoituksesta' is the elative case of 'majoitus' (accommodation). 'Kiitos' + elative case is the standard Finnish way to say 'thank you for...'. A polite phrase when checking out.
